title: What to know before using Angelica sinensis (Dong Quai)?
intro: Angelica sinensis, also known as Dong Quai or Chinese Angelica, is a herbaceous perennial plant native to China. It is a hardy plant that thrives in well-drained soil and full sun, but can also tolerate partial shade. The plant can grow up to 3 feet tall and has hollow, striated stems with wide, bi-pinnate leaves. The small, white or greenish-white flowers are arranged in umbels, and the plant produces brown, oval-shaped seeds. Medicinally, Angelica sinensis is known for its ability to regulate menstrual cycles, alleviate menstrual cramps, and reduce symptoms of menopause. It is also used to boost the immune system, improve circulation, and treat anemia. The root of the plant is used in herbal medicine, and it is rich in vitamins and minerals, including vitamin B12, folic acid, and iron.
medicine_desc: Angelica sinensis, also known as Dong Quai or Chinese Angelica, is a plant that has been used in traditional Chinese medicine for centuries due to its numerous medicinal benefits. It is often used to treat a variety of health conditions, including menstrual cramps, menopause symptoms, and high blood pressure. Additionally, Angelica sinensis has been shown to boost the immune system, reduce inflammation, and improve circulation. Its medicinal properties are attributed to its rich content of vitamins, minerals, and antioxidants.

Angelica sinensis contains various bioactive constituents that contribute to its medicinal properties. These constituents include essential oils, phytosterols, coumarins, and polysaccharides. The essential oil of Angelica sinensis contains substances such as ligustilide, butylidene phthalide, and senkyunolide, which have been shown to have anti-inflammatory, analgesic, and neuroprotective effects. The phytosterols in Angelica sinensis, such as beta-sitosterol and stigmasterol, have been shown to have cholesterol-lowering and immune-enhancing effects. The coumarins in Angelica sinensis, such as osthol and imperatorin, have been shown to have anticoagulant and anticancer effects. The polysaccharides in Angelica sinensis have been shown to have immunomodulatory and antioxidant effects.

Angelica sinensis can be prepared in various forms for medicinal use. The most common form of Angelica sinensis is a dried root, which can be used to make tea or can be ground into a powder for use in capsules or tablets. The root can also be steamed and sliced, then added to soups or stews. Additionally, Angelica sinensis can be found in tincture or extract form, which can be added to water or other beverages. The recommended dosage of Angelica sinensis varies depending on the form and the condition being treated, but it is generally recommended to start with a low dose and gradually increase as needed.

While Angelica sinensis has many medicinal benefits, it can also have side effects, particularly if taken in large doses or for extended periods. Some of the potential side effects of Angelica sinensis include skin irritation, photosensitivity, and digestive issues such as nausea, diarrhea, and stomach pain. Additionally, Angelica sinensis can interact with certain medications, such as blood thinners and antidepressants, and can increase the risk of bleeding. Pregnant or breastfeeding women should avoid using Angelica sinensis, as it may have estrogenic effects and could potentially harm the fetus or infant.

Precautions should be taken when using Angelica sinensis, particularly in certain populations. Individuals with bleeding disorders or who are taking blood thinners should use caution when taking Angelica sinensis, as it may increase the risk of bleeding. Additionally, individuals with estrogen-sensitive conditions, such as breast cancer or endometriosis, should avoid using Angelica sinensis, as it may have estrogenic effects. It is recommended to consult with a healthcare provider before using Angelica sinensis, particularly if taking medication or have a medical condition. Additionally, it is important to follow the recommended dosage and not to use Angelica sinensis for extended periods without consulting a healthcare provider.
horticulture_desc: Angelica sinensis, also known as dong quai or female ginseng, is a herbaceous perennial plant that thrives in cooler climates. It requires partial shade to full sun and well-drained soil. This plant prefers a cooler temperature range of 60-70°F during the growing season and can tolerate frost. It is native to high altitude regions in China, Japan, and Korea, so it is accustomed to cooler temperatures and can even benefit from a period of cold stratification during the winter months.

When planting Angelica sinensis, it is best to start with root divisions or seeds in the early spring. The seeds should be sown in a cold frame or indoors and transplanted outside after the danger of frost has passed. The plants should be spaced 1-2 feet apart and grown in rich, well-drained soil. It is important to keep the soil consistently moist, but not waterlogged, during the growing season.

Angelica sinensis requires regular watering and fertilization to thrive. A balanced, slow-release fertilizer can be applied in the spring to provide necessary nutrients. Regularly check the soil moisture and water the plant if the top inch of soil is dry. Mulching around the base of the plant can help retain moisture and suppress weeds. Deadheading spent flowers will encourage the plant to produce more foliage.

Harvesting Angelica sinensis can be done in the second or third year of growth. The roots are typically harvested in the fall, after the first frost, when the plant's medicinal properties are at their peak. To harvest, carefully dig up the entire plant, including the roots, and gently shake off the excess soil. The roots should be washed and cut into small pieces before drying. The leaves and stems can also be harvested and used fresh or dried.

Angelica sinensis is relatively pest and disease-free, but it can be susceptible to powdery mildew and root rot if the soil is too wet. To prevent these issues, ensure proper drainage and avoid overwatering. Slugs and snails may also be a problem, but they can be controlled with organic pest control methods. Regularly check the plant for any signs of pests or disease and take appropriate action if necessary. With proper care and attention, Angelica sinensis can be a rewarding and valuable addition to any horticultural endeavor.
botany_desc: Angelica sinensis, also known as Dong Quai or Chinese Angelica, is a herbaceous perennial plant that belongs to the family Apiaceae. It is a species of Angelica native to northeast and central China, typically growing in high altitudes of 1000-3000 meters. The plant is taxonomically classified under the genus Angelica, which contains around 60 species of aromatic, tall herbs. Angelica sinensis is a diploid plant, with a chromosome number of 2n=22. It is a close relative of Angelica archangelica, or Garden Angelica, which is native to Europe. However, the two species differ in their morphology, with Angelica sinensis having a more branched stem and a less hollow stem than Angelica archangelica.

Angelica sinensis is a tall, imposing plant that can grow up to 2 meters in height. It has a thick, stout stem that is branched in the upper part, with large, bright green, compound leaves. The leaves are tripinnate, with the ultimate segments oblong-lanceolate and serrate. The inflorescence is a large, compound umbel, with small, white or greenish-white flowers. The fruit is a dry, two-seeded schizocarp, which splits into two mericarps at maturity. The roots of Angelica sinensis are thick and fleshy, with a brown or blackish-brown exterior and a yellowish-white interior.

Angelica sinensis is known by various names, such as Dong Quai, Chinese Angelica, and Female Ginseng. It is often confused with Angelica polymorpha var. sinensis, which is a variant of Angelica that is native to Japan and Korea. However, the two variants differ in their morphology and chemical composition. Angelica sinensis has a higher content of essential oils, while Angelica polymorpha var. sinensis has a higher content of coumarins. Moreover, Angelica sinensis is more branched and has a more hollow stem than Angelica polymorpha var. sinensis.

Angelica sinensis is native to northeast and central China, typically growing in high altitudes of 1000-3000 meters. It prefers moist, well-drained, and fertile soils, with a partial shade. The plant is commonly found in grasslands, meadows, and forest edges. It is also cultivated in some regions of China, such as Gansu, Sichuan, and Yunnan provinces. In recent years, Angelica sinensis has been introduced and cultivated in some countries of Europe and North America, such as France, Germany, and Canada. However, the plant requires specific growing conditions, such as a cool climate and a long growing season, which limit its cultivation in some regions.

Angelica sinensis has a biennial or triennial life-cycle, depending on the environmental conditions and the cultivation practices. In its first year, the plant develops a rosette of leaves and a thick, fleshy root. In the second year, it produces a tall stem, compound leaves, and a large, compound umbel of flowers. The plant then produces seeds and dies, leaving behind the root, which can be harvested and used for medicinal purposes. In some cases, the plant can live for three years, producing seeds in the third year. The root of Angelica sinensis is rich in bioactive compounds, such as essential oils, coumarins, and polysaccharides, which have various medicinal properties, such as anti-inflammatory, immunostimulant, and antioxidant effects.
